insights: Goal completion rate is uniformly 100% across all records and all categorical dimensions (category, location, priority, assignement_group), indicating no variation to explain or differentiate performance.

Negative resolution durations—indicating data entry errors where 'closed_at' precedes 'opened_at'—occur in 48–57% of records within every categorical group, confirming the anomaly is systemic rather than localized to specific teams, categories, or priorities.
The absence of any incomplete goals eliminates the possibility of modeling drivers of non-completion, rendering factor-based impact analysis on completion rates infeasible with this dataset.

Uniform distribution of data quality issues (negative durations) across all groups implies that departmental performance cannot be meaningfully benchmarked, ranked, or diagnosed using this dataset’s current structure or content.

No categorical or temporal dimension exhibits statistically or operationally meaningful variation in either goal completion status or resolution duration reliability—removing all candidate levers for targeted operational intervention.
summary: The dataset provides no basis for analyzing factors influencing goal completion rates, as all 500 records are completed (100% completion rate) with zero variation across time, category, location, priority, or assignement_group. The only measurable anomaly—negative resolution durations—is evenly distributed (48–57% per group), confirming it reflects a systemic data capture issue rather than differential team performance. Consequently, no categorical or temporal factor can be credibly linked to completion outcomes or used to assess departmental performance differentials. Next focus should shift to validating upstream data collection processes and obtaining a dataset with actual variation in goal status or measurable performance differentials.
